coredata.dc:title: Efficient imaging based on P - and N-codoped carbon dots for tracking division and viability assessment of lactic acid bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Tianxin
Wan, Yang
Jin, Furong
Liu, Buwei
Wang, Jindi
Yin, Xinyue
Fu, Xiangbo
Tian, Bo
Feng, Zhibiao
coredata.dc:description: Assessment of lactic acid bacteria (LAB) activity plays a key role in the fermented food industry. Fluorescence imaging method based on dye is facile to detect LAB viability. However, it is difficult to obtain stable fluorescence, non-toxic and low-cost dyes. In this study, we prepare P- and N-doped carbon dots (PN-CDs) via microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis. The properties of high quantum yield (60.36%) and excitation dependence allowed for multicolor imaging of LAB (Lactobacillus plantarum [L.p] and Streptococcus thermophilus [S.t]). The abundant functional groups and positive charges (+2.34 mV) on the surface of PN-CDs facilitated their quickly integrated into cell wall of live LAB with obvious fluorescence or into dead cells. As a result, PN-CDs can not only be used to rapidly and efficiently monitor bacterial viability (one minute), but can also be used to visualize LAB division using fluorescence imaging. Importantly, the PN-CDs have potential to rapidly detect LAB activity in LAB-fermented juices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
LAB viability
Division state
Fermented juices

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Multicolor imaging of LAB with PN-CDs
Verification of the PN-CDs labeling method for distinguishing dead and live LAB
Comparison of the four methods for LAB enumeration
Imaging of L.p in fermented fruit juice using PN-CDs
Multicoclor imaging of LAB with PN-CDs
Verification of the PN-CDs labeling method for distinguishing dead and live LAB
Comparison of the four methods for LAB enumeration
Imaging L.p in fermented fruit juice using PN-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of synthesis and application of PN-CDs.

Optical properties and morphology of PN-CDs. (a) TEM image and particle size distribution. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum (λ em = 463 nm), and emission spectrum (λex = 356 nm) (c) Fluorescence emission spectra under different excitations.
(a) XPS spectrum, high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s, and (e) P 2p, and (f) FTIR spectrum of PN-CDs.
Image of L.p (a) Control sample without PN-CDs, PN-CDs staining (b) UV, (c) blue, and (d) green excitation. Scale bar = 5 µm.
Fluorescence imaging of L.p killed by different methods. PN-CDs (a, b, c, d) UV excitation, PI/FDA (e, f, g, h) blue excitation. Scale bar = 5 µm.
Fluorescence imaging of LAB. L.p sample with (a) PI/FDA, blue excitation, (b) PN-CDs, UV excitation, S.t sample with (c) PI/FDA, blue excitation, (d) PN-CDs, UV excitation. Scale bar = 5 µm.

PN-CDs -LAB interactions. Relative fluorescence intensity of (a) live L.p cells with PN-CDs. (b) dead L.p cells with PN-CDs. Flow cytometry analysis of (c) live L.p cells incubated with PN-CDs for 5 min (d) dead L.p cells incubated with PN-CDs for 5 min (e) Zeta potential analysis of samples. SEM of (f) live L.p cells without PN-CDs. (g) live cells incubated with PN-CDs.
Fluorescence microscopic images of L.p (a) without PN-CDs and (b) with PN-CDs in the background of fermented fruit juice, UV excitation. Scale bar = 5 µm.

head.ce:title: Efficient imaging based on P - and N-codoped carbon dots for tracking division and viability assessment of lactic acid bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin,150030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Northeast Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin,150030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Assessment of lactic acid bacteria (LAB) activity plays a key role in the fermented food industry. Fluorescence imaging method based on dye is facile to detect LAB viability. However, it is difficult to obtain stable fluorescence, non-toxic and low-cost dyes. In this study, we prepare P- and N-doped carbon dots (PN-CDs) via microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis. The properties of high quantum yield (60.36%) and excitation dependence allowed for multicolor imaging of LAB (Lactobacillus plantarum [L.p] and Streptococcus thermophilus [S.t]). The abundant functional groups and positive charges (+2.34 mV) on the surface of PN-CDs facilitated their quickly integrated into cell wall of live LAB with obvious fluorescence or into dead cells. As a result, PN-CDs can not only be used to rapidly and efficiently monitor bacterial viability (one minute), but can also be used to visualize LAB division using fluorescence imaging. Importantly, the PN-CDs have potential to rapidly detect LAB activity in LAB-fermented juices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a representative probiotic, LAB are widely present in traditional fermented foods, such as yogurt, cheese, sourdough, and beverages [1]. In recent years, LAB have been used to make popular fermented juices, which effectively combine the probiotic function of LAB with the nutritional functions of fruits and vegetables, while also imparting new flavors to fruits and vegetables. LAB activity is an important factor that affects the flavor and shelf life of fermented juices, thus, LAB must survive in sufficient numbers during the manufacturing process, storage, and under other environmental stresses. Therefore, real-time monitoring of LAB activity during fermentation is particularly important for determining the quality of fermented juices [2].

Traditional methods of LAB counting include Gram staining and plate counting. Gram staining is less time-consuming; however, it can only quantify the total number of LAB and not distinguish between dead/live LAB. Additionally, it is further restricted because of the complex staining process, fixing bacteria, repeated rinsing sample, and required experienced technical skills. The plate counting method determines the number of viable bacteria in a sample; whereas, it is time-consuming and susceptible to culture conditions. In recent years, a variety of rapid bacterial detection instruments have been developed, including flow cytometry [3], FTIR spectroscopy [4] and Raman scattering[5]. However, these instruments require considerable time and money for maintenance; thus, they cannot be widely used. At present, fluorescent dye staining combined with fluorescence microscopy is emerging as a routine method to monitor microorganism viability, which is necessary for food safety and human health. Propidium iodide (PI), a common commercial chemical dye, penetrates damaged cell membranes to identify dead cells [6]. Furthermore, positively charged asymmetric monomethylcyanine, negatively charged oxonol (DiBAC4(3)) and ethidium bromide also quickly label dead cells [7,8]. However, these fluorescent dyes are usually highly cytotoxic and expensive and require repeated washing during staining.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial with excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties [9]. They have been widely applied in biomonitoring, sensors, bio-imaging, photocatalysis, and drug and gene delivery [10]. Compared to traditional fluorescent semiconductor nanomaterials, CDs have many advantages, such as stable fluorescence properties [11,12], good biocompatibility [13], low biotoxicity [14], low environmental hazards [15], and tunable surface chemistry [16].

Multiple methods have been used to synthesize CDs, including hydrothermal [17], solvothermal [18], pyrolysis [19], ultrasonication [20], and microwave-assisted methods [21]. Among them, the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method has gained the most attention because of its simple operation, short preparation time, and green nature. In the hydrothermal process, the precursor is a core factor that affects the fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) and emission properties of the obtained CDs [22]. Because of its abundant hydroxyl groups and ability to provide a carbon source, citric acid (CA) has become an excellent precursor for CDs [23]. The amino group on the surface of ethylenediamine (EDA) and carboxyl group of CA can be dehydrated to form an amide group and then condensed to form a stable cross-linked polymer. CDs synthesized using CA and EDA exhibit strong fluorescence stability, high fluorescence QYs, and easy modification. By doping with heteroatoms can improve properties of CDs, and these elements include sulfur [24], chlorine [25], fluorine [26], boron [27,28], and phosphorus [29], et al. In recent years, phosphorus (P) has attracted increasing attention. Phosphorus improves the fluorescence lifetime and stability of CDs, and excited phosphorescence can penetrate tissues for biological imaging [30]. Additionally, the electronegativity of phosphorus is lower than that of carbon atoms, resulting in the formation of a positive charge distribution around carbon atoms. Positively charged CDs better absorb negatively charged biomolecules, which is conducive for cell imaging [31].

Presently, some CDs reported could distinguish dead and alive cells though staining dead cells[32–36]. This is mainly because the increased cell permeability of dead cells allows CDs to enter dead cells, while the live cannot bind to CDs due to electrostatic repulsion and other effects, and live cells cannot be observed. These CDs enable precise labeling of dead cells, but are not suitable for microbial enumeration. And CDs in microscopic imaging mostly focus on yeast [37], Escherichia coli [38], and Staphylococcus aureus [39], while LAB imaging has not been reported. Therefore, to develop a method to rapidly stain live LAB and distinguish dead/live cells is highly desired for improving the quality of fermented foods.

In this study, we used CA and EDA as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, and increased fluorescence QYs by introducing phosphoric acid to prepare P- and N-doped carbon dots (PN-CDs). As novel fluorescent probes, PN-CDs displayed high fluorescence QYs, low toxicity, and excellent fluorescence stability. Furthermore, the abundant functional groups (hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups) on the surface of PN-CDs promoted their interactions with LAB. The PN-CDs could not only be used to distinguish dead/live cells, but also monitor the viability of LAB in fermented juices through fluorescence imaging.

CA, EDA, phosphoric acid, sodium hydroxide, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). De Man Rogosa Sharpe (MRS), M17 medium, and dialysis bags (1000 Da) were purchased from Beijing Solabo Biological Co., Ltd, Ltd. (Beijing, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, Fluorescein diacetate (FDA) were obtained from Mclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PI and ammonium oxalate crystal solution, iodine solution, and safranin were supplied by Shanghai Biyuntian Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

PN-CDs were prepared using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method according to a previously reported method with minor modification [40]. CA (5 g), EDA (2 mL), and phosphoric acid (3 mL) were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water. The mixed solution was transferred to a microwave-assisted synthesizer (XH-300A+; Beijing Xianhu Technology Development Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) and reacted at 180 ℃ for 1 h. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at a high speed (12850 × g) for 15 min to remove impurity. The supernatant was dialyzed in dialysis bags for 72 h and then lyophilized.

A JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operating at 200 kV was used to analyze the morphology of the PN-CDs. The ultraviolet (UV)-visual absorption spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550; Shimadzu Ltd., Kyoto, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the PN-CDs were recorded using a TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). X-ray was used to analysis the element composition with a X-electron spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Chigasaki, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Horiba Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan).

The fluorescence QYs of the PN-CDs were calculated according to a previously reported method [41]. Using quinine sulfate (QY = 55%) as the reference, the relative fluorescence QYs of the PN-CDs were calculated using the following formula:
(1)
QY =
Q
R
×
I
/
I
R
A
R
/
A
n
/
n
R
2
where I is the integral area of the emission peak intensity corresponding to the excitation wavelength, n is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the absorbance, and the subscript R is the reference substance.

To study the effect of pH on PN-CDs, PN-CDs solutions (200 µg/mL) with different pH values (1−14) were prepared and the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm (λex = 330 nm) was recorded using a spectrofluorometer. To investigate the effect of ion concentration on PN-CDs, the fluorescence intensities of PN-CDs (200 µg/mL) in different buffer concentrations and different ions (1 M) were recorded. Moreover, different sugars (1 M) vitamin (1 M) and proteins (1 M) were added into the PN-CDs solution, and measured under operating conditions. For the UV irradiation experiment, the PN-CDs solution was placed under a UV lamp (365 nm, 20 mW) for 120 min and its fluorescence intensities were recorded every 20 min. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the PN-CDs after storage in the dark for 30 d was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor imaging of LAB with PN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the imaging study of LAB, Lactobacillus plantarum (L.p) and Streptococcus thermophilus (S.t) were cultured in MRS and M17 media for 12 h, respectively. The culture medium was discarded by centrifugation to collect the LAB, which were then resuspended in a phosphate buffer solution. The LAB samples were treated with 200 µg/mL PN-CDs solution for 1 min without water washing. After the samples were fixed, they were imaged via a fluorescence microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany) (UV, 350 nm excitation, 450 nm emission; blue light, 480 nm excitation, 527 nm emission; and green light, 535 nm excitation, 590 nm emission).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the PN-CDs labeling method for distinguishing dead and live LAB
ce:section.ce:para: Using the PI/FDA staining method, each bacterial solution sample was stained with 30 μM PI at 4 ℃ for 3 min and washed repeatedly, then stained with 10 μg/mL FDA in the dark at 37 ℃ for 20 min, washed repeatedly, and finally imaged using a fluorescence microscope. L.p and S.t were killed using sequential heating, microwaves, ethanol, and formaldehyde treatments. The samples were labeled using PN-CDs and PI/FDA before and after treatment, respectively. In addition, we prepared samples with mortality rates of 20%, 50% and 80%, and stained them with PN-CDs and PI/FDA respectively, then imaged the samples with a fluorescence microscope, compared the death rate counted by the two dyeing methods.

A portion of the cultured L.p cells was killed using ethanol. PN-CDs were added to a series of live/dead L.p cell suspensions (optical density [OD] 600 = 0–2) to obtain a PN-CDs concentration of 10 µg/mL. The fluorescence spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

Furthermore, 10,000 cells were collected from each sample and the mean fluorescence intensity of dead/live cells labeled with PN-CDs was measured using a flow cytometer (Becton Dickinson, NY, USA) at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. The zeta potentials of the PN-CDs, dead/live L.p cells, and dead/live L.p cells treated with PN-CDs were also measured.

To more intuitively observe whether the binding of PN-CDs to L.p cells affects cell integrity, we investigated the morphological changes of live L.p cells before and after PN-CDs treatment using scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

The growth curve of L.p was plotted by cultivating L.p in MRS medium containing different concentrations of PN-CDs (0, 10, 20, 40, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL) or 30 μM PI at 37 °C for 24 h. S.t was cultured in M17 medium at 42 °C for 12 h, and other treatment conditions were the same as those of L.p.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the four methods for LAB enumeration
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the accuracy of PN-CDs in LAB enumeration, L.p was counted using the plate counting method, Gram staining method, PI/FDA, and PN-CDs staining.

For the plate counting method: L.p was added to MRS medium at a 1% bacterial dose, cultured for 18 h, and then the bacterial solution was diluted–105-107 times. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 48 h.

For the Gram staining method, 5 μL of diluted bacterial solution was smeared on a square of 1 cm2 on a microscope slide. After all L.p cells were fixed, the bacteria were treated with ammonium oxalate crystal violet for 1 min and then washed with water. The iodine solution was added dropwise for mordant dyeing for 1 min and then washed with water. Finally, the sample was decolorized using 95% ethanol for 30 s and then washed with water. The number of bacteria in 30 fields of view was randomly counted using a microscope, and the average number of L.p in each field of view was obtained.
Similarly, 5 μL of the diluted bacterial solution was stained using PI/FDA and PN-CDs. The staining method was the same as above, and the counting method was based on the Gram staining method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of L.p in fermented fruit juice using PN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practicality of PN-CDs as a fluorescent probe, fermented apple juice was prepared with L.p. 1 mL suspension of L.p cells (1 × 106 CFU/mL) was added to 100 mL of apple juice and then incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The sample treated with 200 μg/mL PN-CDs was smeared on a microscope slide. The samples were imaged excitation using a fluorescence microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany).

A one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with three measurements was used to evaluate differences between the signal among the tested samples and controls. All the statistics analysis was conducted with a commercial software from Statistical Product and Service Solutions (SPSS) Inc.
A general scheme of the preparation process and application of PN-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1.

The morphology of the PN-CDs was characterized using TEM ( Fig. 2a). The PN-CDs were well-dispersed and uniform in size, and the particle size was concentrated between 2 and 4 nm. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peak at 240 nm was assigned to the π = π * transition of the CC bond [41]. In addition, in the lower energy region, the absorption peak at 345 nm was ascribed to the surface groups CO and CN [42]. We measured the emission spectra of the PN-CDs at excitation wavelengths of 310–460 nm (Fig. 2c). When the excitation wavelength was greater than 380 nm, the emission intensity of the PN-CDs decreased significantly, and a red shift occurred with an increase in the excitation wavelength. Compared with that of the N-CDs (Fig. S1), the PN-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent luminescence properties caused by different surface states and energy levels of the CDs [43]. Similar phenomena were observed for other CDs, which was consistent with their application in multicolor imaging [44]. Furthermore, the fluorescence QY of the PN-CDs was determined as 60.36%, which was higher than that of P-free NCDs (50.32%). The average fluorescence lifetime of the PN-CDs was 14.66 ns, which was 2.97 ns higher than that of N-CDs. (Fig. S2).

In the XPS spectrum, characteristic peaks were observed at 199.6 eV, 285.3 eV, 403.9 eV, and 534.6 eV ( Fig. 3a), representing P, C, N, and O, respectively. The atom ratios of the elements are 3%, 62%, 16%, and 19%. Four peaks from C1s spectrum at 284.6 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.6 eV, attributed to CC, C-O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 3b) [45]. For the XPS peak of O1s (Fig. 3c), two fitting peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.1 eV corresponding to CO and C-O, respectively, were observed [46]. The spectrum of N1s exhibited a peak at 399.5 eV and 402.2 eV, which were likely attributed to N-H and N-C, respectively (Fig. 3d) [47]. The peak at 133.2 eV was consistent with the P-O structure, and the peak at 134.2 eV was related to the P-C group of the PN-CDs (Fig. 3e) [48]. The surface functional groups of the PN-CDs were further investigated using FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3f, the peaks at 3425 cm−1, 2929 cm−1, and 1392 cm−1 correspond to the O-H/-NH2, C-H, and C-O bonds on the surface of the PN-CDs, respectively [49]. These hydrophilic groups effectively improve the dispersibility and stability of CDs in water, which is beneficial for their application in biological imaging [50]. The absorption peaks at 1652 cm−1, 1219 cm−1, and 775 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of C-N, P-O, and P-C, respectively [51]. The XPS and FTIR spectra confirmed that P and N atoms were successfully doped into the PN-CDs.

For practical applications, it is important to understand the photostability of PN-CDs under different conditions. Therefore, the fluorescence stability of PN-CDs under different pH values, ion concentrations, long-time UV lamp irradiation, and dark conditions was investigated. As shown in Fig. S3a, the fluorescence of the PN-CDs showed high stability in the pH range of 3–8. The fluorescence intensity remained above 70% even at pH values of 2 and 10. The acid and alkaline resistance of the PN-CDs fluorescence allows labeling of LAB under extreme environmental conditions, such as in the gastrointestinal tract. The fluorescence intensity of the PN-CDs decreased slightly with increasing ionic strength (Fig. S3b). As shown in Figs. S3c and d, the PN-CDs showed excellent long-term stability under dark conditions and did not significantly change under continuous UV irradiation for 120 min. Moreover, considering the practical applications of the PN-CDs in food products, the fluorescence stability of PN-CDs under different ions, proteins vitamin and sugar was determined (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). These results indicate that the PN-CDs exhibit high fluorescence stability and can be widely used in LAB imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicoclor imaging of LAB with PN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence microscopy images of LAB with PN-CDs (200 µg/mL) are shown in Fig. 4 and S6. Each LAB cell was clearly labeled and multicolor-imaged, demonstrating the applicability of PN-CDs as fluorescent probes for LAB imaging. Furthermore, the division state of LAB was clearly observed (box, Fig. 4b and Fig. S6b). A highly fluorescent domain was observed in the middle of the dividing cells, which may be attributed to the local fluorescence enhancement associated with membrane-associated proteins moving toward the center and PN-CDs binding to membrane-associated proteins when LAB cells divide [52–54]. It is of great significance for understanding the division mode of LAB and mastering their biological behavior of LAB. In addition, we found that there were two types of LAB images. One was a bright blue whole cell (circle, Fig. 4b and Fig. S6b), and the other was cell walll with obvious fluorescence, which might be related to the living state of the LAB cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the PN-CDs labeling method for distinguishing dead and live LAB
ce:section.ce:para: L.p and S.t were killed using water bath heating and ethanol, formaldehyde, and microwave treatment. Lethal LAB samples were stained with PI/FDA and PN-CDs. PI can pass through the membranes of dead cells to emit red fluorescence. FDA is transformed into a green, fluorescent metabolite after absorption by living cells, causing living cells to emit green fluorescence. For samples labeled with PI/FDA, all LAB cells were red, indicating that they were dead after treatment ( Fig. 5e-h and Fig. S7e-h). For samples labeled with PN-CDs, the whole cell was bright blue, demonstrating that dead LAB cells emitted bright blue fluorescence when stained with PN-CDs (Fig. 5a-d and Fig. S7a-d). Cultured LAB were then stained with PI/FDA and PN-CDs. For samples labeled with PI/FDA, the green cells were alive, whereas the red cells were dead ( Fig. 6a, c). For the same samples labeled with PN-CDs, LAB cells showed two different states. We confirmed that dead cells appeared bright blue while living cells wall with a bright halo. In addition, samples with lethal rates of 20%, 50%, and 80% were stained with PI/FDA and PN-CDs respectively. Due to the different field of view, the number of dead and live bacteria stained by the two methods was slightly different (Fig. S8). We selected 100 visual fields to count the mortality of LAB. The results obtained by PI/FDA staining method were 17.34% ± 1.15a, 46.17% ± 0.96a, and 78.29% ± 1.03a, respectively. And the results of PN-CDs staining were 18.31% ± 1.06a, 46.21% ± 1.17a, and 76.46% ± 1.12a, respectively. Analysis of variance showed that there was no significant difference between the two test results (p > 0.05), indicating that PN-CDs staining method was accurate and reliable.

Peptidoglycan and lipoteichoic acid in the LAB cell wall provide anionic sites, which can promote the binding of the amino groups in PN-CDs to cells. The abundant hydrophilic groups on the peptidoglycan surface also form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the PN-CDs. Furthermore, The cell membrane is a semipermeable lipid bilayer, through which only certain small molecules can diffuse into the interior of the cell. As the size of the PN-CDs was concentrated at 2–4 nm, PN-CDs did not enter the LAB cells quickly or in large quantities. Therefore, in live LAB cells treated with PN-CDs, only the outer wall fluoresced. The membranes of dead LAB cells were destroyed, which significantly increased cell permeability, such that PN-CDs quickly entered the dead cells in a short time and induced bright blue fluorescence in the dead LAB cells. It provides a possibility for PN-CDs to identify the dead or alive of other bacteria, such as gram-positive bacteria whose cell walls are rich in peptidoglycans. This is similar to the results of Peng's team [55].

We compared the binding rates of PN-CDs to a series of dead/live L.p cells at different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was stronger with the increase in cell concentration, and compared with that of live cells, dead cells exhibited higher fluorescence intensity (The illustration showed the fluorescence spectrum of the treated sample). The fluorescence intensity of dead and live cells treated with PN-CDs was quantitatively analyzed using flow cytometry (Fig. 7c and d). Compared with that of untreated cells, the fluorescence intensity of dead and live cells treated with PN-CDs increased, and the fluorescence intensity of dead cells was significantly higher than that of living cells. These results confirmed that the PN-CDs bind to L.p cells. After treatment with PN-CDs, the dead cells showed a higher fluorescence intensity, which was related to the entrance of PN-CDs in dead cells. This is consistent with the fluorescence microscopy imaging results.

The surface charge plays a crucial role in the interaction between CDs and bacteria [56]. We speculated that electrostatic adsorption between LAB cells and PN-CDs may be one of the main determinants of simultaneous labeling of live/dead LAB cells by PN-CDs, As shown in Fig. 7e, PN-CDs displayed a positive charge (+2.34 mV), which may be attributed to the presence of multiple amino groups on the surface of the PN-CDs. Live and dead LAB (L.p and S.t) cells were negatively charged, and the electronegativity of dead/live L.p and S.t cells treated with PN-CDs was reduced compared with that of untreated cells, indicating that PN-CDs combined with the L.p cells.

To more intuitively observe the binding state of PN-CDs with cells, we imaged living L.p cells using SEM. As shown in Fig. 7f and g, the surface of the untreated L.p cells was smooth, and the cell morphology was intact. After co-incubation with the PN-CDs, several aggregated particles on the cell wall surface were observed, but the cells remained intact. This indicates that PN-CDs bind to cells and do not damage them.

As PI staining is the most widely used staining method for dead bacteria, we compared the toxicity of PN-CDs and PI using the plate count method and growth curves of L.p and S.t. The plate counting results demonstrate that the number of colonies of L.p and S.t treated with 30 μM PI (working concentration) was significantly reduced, while the number of colonies of L.p and S.t treated with 400 µg/mL PN-CDs (substantially greater than the working concentration) did not significantly decrease compared with those in the control (Figure. S9a).

Moreover, the growth curves of L.p and S.t did not significantly change when the concentration of PN-CDs was increased, whereas the OD of L.p and S.t decreased significantly after PI treatment compared with that in the control (Fig. S9b and c). These results indicate that PN-CDs are significantly less toxic than PI and thus are suitable as environmentally friendly biofluorescent-labeled probes for LAB staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the four methods for LAB enumeration
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy of LAB enumeration using PN-CDs, the plate count and direct microscopic methods (including Gram, PI/FDA, and PN-CDs staining) were used to count L.p (Table S1). No significant differences were observed between PN-CDs staining and the other counting methods (P > 0.05). Compared with that of Gram staining, the total number of L.p cells counted using PN-CDs staining was slightly higher, which may be due to excessive washing during the Gram staining method. Moreover, PN-CDs simultaneously counted live and dead L.p, whereas Gram staining only counted total L.p. Compared with PN-CDs staining, PI/FDA staining was more convenient for quantification because it could distinguish live/dead LAB with different colors, while PN-CDs staining distinguished live/dead LAB by the distribution of PN-CDs on LAB. However, the number of live and dead L.p counted using PN-CDs staining was slightly higher than that of PI/FDA staining, which may be attributed to the repeated washing and centrifugation steps during the PI/FDA staining process, resulting in the loss of L.p. Furthermore, PI/FDA staining was more toxic to cells than PN-CDs staining and the staining time required strict regulation. For viable counts, the PN-CDs staining result was closest to that of plate counting. However, plate counting requires 48 h of culture and only counts live L.p, whereas the PN-CDs staining method directly counts live and dead L.p, shortens detection time, and rapidly detects L.p viability. These results further revealed that PN-CDs staining for enumerating LAB demonstrates high accuracy, practicability, and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging L.p in fermented fruit juice using PN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: PN-CDs were used to image L.p in fermented apple juice ( Fig. 8). Apple juice fermentation samples can be directly stained with PN-CDs to detect L.p activity. It can be seen from the figure that live and dead L.p cells were well distinguished, indicating that PN-CDs could still be stably combined with LAB in the fermented juice system, and the components of fermented juice had no effect on the fluorescence of PN-CDs. Therefore, PN-CDs have the potential to be used as fluorescent probes to detect LAB activity in fermented beverages.

In this study, we prepared PN-CDs and applied them to LAB imaging. The PN-CDs displayed high water solubility, dispersibility, fluorescence stability, and fluorescence QY. PN-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent optical properties and can be used for three-color imaging of LAB. Fluorescence spectroscopy, flow cytometry, SEM, and other methods were used to demonstrate the stable combination of the PN-CDs and LAB cell imaging. Moreover, PN-CDs distinguished between dead/live LAB and tracked the division state of LAB. Compared with other methods, PN-CDs staining for enumerating L.p is simple, fast, accurate, and can be applied to monitor the viability of LAB in fermented fruit juice. Therefore, PN-CDs can be used as environmentally friendly probes for the rapid detection of LAB count and activity.
The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2019C020).

Tianxin Fu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yang Wan: Validation, Formal analysis, Software. Furong Jin: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Buwei Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Jindi Wang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Xinyue Yin: Writing – review & editing. Xiangbo Fu: Writing – review & editing. Bo Tian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhibiao Feng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
